summary: Yegge reviews The Elder Scrolls IV: Oblivion, a massively addictive single-player RPG that he's been playing instead of blogging. Despite frequent crashes (which he attributes to C++), ugly NPC faces, and repetitive voice acting, he finds the game exceptional due to its beautiful scenery, smart monster AI, and immersive single-player experience. He argues that single-player games have distinct advantages over MMORPGs because they're focused entirely on the player and don't require dealing with toxic multiplayer communities or subscription fees.
key_points: Oblivion is so addictive that real life starts looking like a graphics engine, and players develop genuine aversion to reality
The game crashes frequently, which Yegge uses as evidence that C++ remains problematic for game development
Despite improved graphics, NPCs are disturbingly ugly with realistic but unflattering facial details, and voice acting is limited and melodramatic
Monster AI is genuinely impressive - enemies use evasion tactics, chase players across zones, and will fight other NPCs or monsters
Single-player RPGs have inherent advantages over MMORPGs: no subscriptions, no toxic players, actual plot, and the story is about YOU
money_quotes: The game is so damned addictive that when you're not playing, you wind up looking outside and thinking 'man, that lighting model is really realistic!' before you remember you're not actually playing at that particular moment.
Bethesda called this latest incarnation 'Oblivion' because it sounded better than 'core dump' or 'segmentation fault', but it has the same basic connotations.
Crashes? *gasp* -- I bet you'll never guess what programming language it's written in.
Morrowind was a hideous island dominated by volcanoes and disease storms and mud flats and rotting undead zombies, much like a C++ users convention.
The game's supposed to be an escape from reality, but at times it feels more like an escape to Arkansas.
When you hear an NPC talking about the exploits of some hero, you know it's about you. You don't have to deal with all the assholes you run into in multiplayer games.
Oblivion is a crashy game. It's a case study in why the world shouldn't be using C++.
I highly, nay strongly recommend that you don't start, or you'll suddenly develop an aversion to Real Life, and who knows how long it'll last. Probably until you're fired.
key_insight: Even deeply flawed games can be incredibly compelling when they focus on making the player the hero of their own story, which is something multiplayer games fundamentally cannot do.
